narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from past students—like their grades, test scores, family background, and study plans—to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated your risk of failure at 66.3%, which crossed the threshold for denial. Your profile includes a solid Year 1 decile of 5th place among your peers (a bit below the typical 5.71 average), but a much lower 1st-place decile in Year 3 (compared to the usual 5.53 average), an undergraduate GPA of 2.80 (under the 3.21 average), an LSAT score of 34 (below the 36.70 average), upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time like most students. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 academic decile, as it stood out as unusually low and signaled potential struggles ahead on the bar exam. On a more positive note, your Year 1 decile actually nudged the model toward seeing you as more likely to pass, showing early promise that helped balance things somewhat. Your LSAT score also worked in your favor by suggesting stronger-than-expected potential despite the overall risk. Smaller influences, like your undergraduate GPA and full-time plans, slightly tipped toward higher failure risk, while your family income provided a minor boost toward success. Compared to typical students who get approved, your Year 3 performance was a key outlier dragging you below the bar, even as other areas like your early grades held you closer to average. This isn't a judgment on your worth or potential—models like this reflect historical patterns, and as a Black woman, you may have overcome systemic hurdles that aren't fully captured here. To move forward, focus on strengthening your later academic record, perhaps by retaking the LSAT or building standout experiences in law-related activities, then reapply to schools with holistic reviews that value your unique story. You've got resilience on your side; use this feedback to chart a stronger path to the bar and beyond. Keep pushing—you belong in law.
